walkthrough.title: Choose Ship Boards
walkthrough.text: Let's get Galaxy Trucker set up. Each player takes a Class I space ship board. These are the boards labeled with a big Roman numeral 'I'. You will see a grid of squares on the board with one marked square in the center. That center square is where your starting component goes. For fewer than four players, leave the extra ship boards in the box.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Starting Component
walkthrough.text: Each player gets a starting component in their color. This is your pilot cabin, and it has four universal connectors, one on each side. Place it on the center square of your ship board, the one marked with 'I'. This cabin will be the heart of your ship and the easiest piece to build around since universal connectors can join with any other connector type.

walkthrough.title: Build the Warehouse
walkthrough.text: Now take all the other ship components, the square tiles with pictures of cabins, engines, cannons, cargo holds, and other spaceship parts, and turn them all face down. Mix them up thoroughly in a big pile in the center of the table where everyone can reach them. This pile is called the Warehouse. Think of it as a junkyard full of spaceship parts you are going to rummage through.

walkthrough.title: Place the Number Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the numbered tiles, 1 through 4 for four players, 1 through 3 for three players, or 1 and 2 for two players. Place them in a row near the Warehouse. These tiles determine the launch order when players finish building their ships. The first player to finish grabs tile number 1 and gets to be in front during the flight.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Flight Board
walkthrough.text: You do not need the flight board during the building phase, but go ahead and set it nearby. The flight board has a circle of triangular spaces around the edge, which is the track your ship markers will move along. In the center is a price list for goods and a summary of the round rules. You will use this after building is done.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Ship Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes two space ship markers in their color. Keep one in front of you as a spare. The other one will go on the flight board once everyone has finished building. The markers show your relative position during the flight, which matters a lot for who gets first pick at opportunities and who gets attacked first.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Adventure Cards
walkthrough.text: For your very first game, find the eight adventure cards from the deck labeled 'I' that have an asterisk in the lower left corner. These are the beginner cards that ensure you encounter every type of event. Shuffle them and set them near the flight board. In a full game, you will make three piles of cards placed on the flight board that players can peek at during building, plus a hidden fourth pile shuffled in after building ends.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Bank
walkthrough.text: Dump out all the figures, blocks, markers, and dice from the bag. This is the bank. You will find white human crew figures, purple and orange alien figures, green battery tokens, colored cargo blocks in yellow, green, blue, and red, the two dice, and the cosmic credit tiles. Keep these accessible but out of the way. You will pull from the bank when preparing your ship for launch and during the flight.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Cosmic Credits
walkthrough.text: Take the cosmic credit tiles and place them in neat stacks near the bank. These are the currency of the game. You earn credits by arriving first, selling goods, and completing adventures. You lose credits for lost components. Keep your personal credits face down in a stack so other players cannot see your total. The player with the most credits at the end of Round 3 wins.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Build
walkthrough.text: That is it for setup. Each player should have their Class I ship board with the starting pilot cabin in the center, the Warehouse pile should be face down in the middle of the table, the number tiles should be in a row, and the bank should be accessible. When everyone is ready, the boldest player shouts 'Go!' and the real-time building phase begins. Good luck in the Warehouse!

bullets: Each player takes a Class I ship board (labeled 'I')
For fewer than 4 players, leave extras in the box

bullets: Place your colored pilot cabin on the center square marked 'I'

This pile is the Warehouse

These determine launch order — first finished gets tile 1

Center shows price list and round rules summary

One goes on flight board after building, one stays in front of you

Shuffle and place near the flight board

Includes: white humans, purple/orange aliens, green batteries, colored cargo blocks, 2 dice

summary.title: Prepare Cosmic Credits
bullets: Stack cosmic credit tiles near the bank

summary.title: Ready to Build
bullets: Ship board with pilot cabin in center, Warehouse face down, number tiles in a row
Boldest player shouts 'Go!' to start real-time building

walkthrough.title: Game Overview: Three Rounds
walkthrough.text: Galaxy Trucker is played over three rounds. In each round you first build a space ship in real time, then fly it through a series of adventures. Round 1 uses small Class I ships on a short, safe journey. Round 2 uses bigger Class II ships on a more dangerous trip. Round 3 uses massive Class III ships for the longest, most perilous voyage. The goal is to end the game with the most cosmic credits after all three rounds.

walkthrough.title: Building Phase: Grabbing Components
walkthrough.text: When someone says 'Go!' everyone grabs components from the Warehouse simultaneously. There are no turns. You grab one face-down component at a time with one hand, your other hand must stay above your ship board. Place the component on your board and flip it face up. You can either add it to your ship or return it face up to the center of the table. Once a component is returned face up, anyone can grab it instead of taking a face-down tile. You cannot peek at face-down tiles on the table.

walkthrough.title: Building Phase: Connector Rules
walkthrough.text: Every component has connectors on its edges. There are three types: simple connectors, which look like a single pipe, double connectors, which look like two pipes, and universal connectors, which connect to anything. The key rule is that a simple connector can join with another simple or a universal, and a double can join with another double or a universal. A simple connector cannot join with a double connector. Smooth sides, the edges with no connector at all, cannot join with any connector. If a new piece touches your ship on multiple sides, every touching side must make a legal connection.

walkthrough.title: Building Phase: Component Types
walkthrough.text: The Warehouse contains many types of components. Cabins hold crew members, two per cabin. Engines propel your ship and must point toward the rear with nothing behind the exhaust pipe. Cannons defend against threats and must have nothing in front of the barrel. Cargo holds store goods in 2 or 3 containers. Special cargo holds have reinforced containers for hazardous red goods. Batteries power double engines, double cannons, and shields. Shield generators protect two sides of your ship from small meteors and light cannon fire. Structural modules have lots of connectors and help hold your ship together.

walkthrough.title: Building Phase: Finishing Your Ship
walkthrough.text: When you are happy with your ship, or realize the components you need are gone, grab the lowest available number tile. Tile 1 means you launch first and get first pick at opportunities. You do not have to fill every square. In the full game, there is a sand timer that limits building time. When it runs out, someone can flip it to the next circle, and when it runs out on circle 1, a player with a number tile can flip it to the Start circle for a final countdown. When that runs out, everyone stops building immediately and grabs whatever number tile they can.

walkthrough.title: Spot Check and Common Mistakes
walkthrough.text: After building, everyone checks each other's ships for illegal construction. Common mistakes include a simple connector next to a double, any connector next to a smooth edge, an engine not pointing toward the rear, a component behind an engine exhaust or in front of a cannon barrel, a component outside the building area, or a disconnected section. In the full game, illegal components must be removed and placed in your discard pile, and you pay a penalty for them. In your first game, just fix mistakes by removing the offending piece.

walkthrough.title: Preparing for Launch
walkthrough.text: After the spot check, prepare your ship. Place 2 white human figures in each cabin on your ship, including the pilot cabin. Place 2 or 3 green battery tokens on each battery component matching the number of cells shown. In the full game with aliens, a cabin joined to a life support system can hold either 2 humans or 1 alien of the matching color, but you can only have one alien of each color on your ship. Then place ship markers on the flight board with the player holding tile 1 in front, each subsequent player two spaces behind the previous one.

walkthrough.title: The Flight: Adventure Cards
walkthrough.text: The leader, the player whose marker is farthest ahead, flips the first adventure card. Cards are resolved one at a time. There are many types: Open Space lets you race ahead based on engine strength. Planets let you land to collect goods but cost flight days. Abandoned Ships and Stations let one player trade crew or time for credits or goods. Meteoric Swarms damage ships with small and large meteors. Enemies like Smugglers, Slavers, and Pirates attack ships in order. Combat Zones penalize the weakest player in crew, engines, and cannons. Special Events like Stardust and Epidemic create unique challenges.

walkthrough.title: Open Space and Engine Strength
walkthrough.text: When an Open Space card appears, each player announces their engine strength starting with the leader. Each simple engine counts as 1. Each double engine counts as 2 but you must spend a green battery token to power it. An orange alien adds plus 2 to engine strength if you already have at least 1 engine strength. You move your ship marker forward that many empty spaces, skipping occupied spaces. Faster ships get farther ahead, which matters for who gets first pick at planets and who gets attacked first by enemies.

walkthrough.title: Planets and Goods
walkthrough.text: A Planets card shows 2 to 4 rows of colored goods blocks. The leader chooses first whether to land, then each subsequent player in order. Only one player can land per row. Landing costs flight days, moving your marker backward. You take the colored goods blocks shown and place them in your cargo containers. Yellow goods are worth 1 credit, green are worth 2, blue are worth 3, and red are worth 4 but can only go in special cargo holds. If you have more goods than containers, dump the excess. You can rearrange cargo whenever you load new goods.

walkthrough.title: Abandoned Ships and Stations
walkthrough.text: An Abandoned Ship card lets one player trade crew members for cosmic credits, plus a loss of flight days. The leader decides first. If they pass, the opportunity falls to the next player. Only one player can claim it. An Abandoned Station works similarly but requires a minimum crew count to dock. You gain goods instead of credits and lose flight days. These are valuable opportunities, especially in later rounds where the rewards are bigger.

walkthrough.title: Meteoric Swarms
walkthrough.text: A Meteoric Swarm card shows several meteors coming from specific directions. For each meteor, the leader rolls two dice to determine which row or column it hits. Small meteors bounce off your ship harmlessly unless they hit an exposed connector. If they do, you can block with a shield on that side by spending 1 battery token. Otherwise, the component is destroyed. Large meteors damage your ship no matter what, and shields cannot stop them. Your only defense is a cannon pointing at the meteor in the same column or row. Destroyed components are placed in your discard pile, and anything disconnected from your ship is also lost.

walkthrough.title: Enemies: Smugglers, Slavers, Pirates
walkthrough.text: Enemy cards attack ships in order starting with the leader. You compare your cannon strength to the enemy strength shown on the card. Each forward-pointing simple cannon counts as 1. Each forward-pointing double cannon costs 1 battery token and counts as 2. Side and rear cannons count at half value. A purple alien adds plus 2 if you already have cannon strength. If your strength exceeds the enemy, you win and claim rewards. If you tie, nothing happens. If you lose, you suffer the penalty shown, such as losing goods, crew, or taking cannon fire. If someone wins, the enemy is defeated and stops attacking.

walkthrough.title: Combat Zones
walkthrough.text: A Combat Zone card has three lines evaluated one at a time. First, the player with the fewest crew loses 3 flight days. Second, the player with the weakest engine strength loses 2 crew members. Third, the player with the weakest cannon strength gets hit by light and heavy cannon fire from behind. For each comparison, if multiple players tie for weakest, the one farthest ahead pays the penalty. Players announce and decide on double engine and cannon spending in order starting with the leader.

walkthrough.title: Special Events
walkthrough.text: Yellow adventure cards are special events with unique effects written on the card. Stardust causes every player to lose 1 flight day per exposed connector on their ship, starting with the last player. In Round 2 or 3, Epidemic removes 1 crew member from every occupied cabin that is joined to another occupied cabin. In Round 3, Sabotage blows up a random component on the ship with the smallest crew by rolling dice for coordinates. These events make ship design choices really matter.

walkthrough.title: Taking Hits and Losing Components
walkthrough.text: When a component is destroyed, remove it from your ship and place it in your discard pile. If removing it disconnects other components from your ship, those are lost too. If your ship breaks into two pieces, you choose which piece to keep. All crew, batteries, and goods on lost components go back to the bank. This is why building a solid, connected ship is so important. One well-placed hit can cause a chain reaction that tears your ship apart.

walkthrough.title: Giving Up
walkthrough.text: In the full game, you might have to give up. You must give up if you lose all your human crew, if your engine strength is zero during Open Space, or if the leader laps you on the flight board. You can also choose to give up before the next adventure card is revealed. If you give up, remove your marker, skip all remaining adventures, get no arrival bonuses, and sell your goods at half price. Sometimes cutting your losses early is the smart move.

walkthrough.title: Journey's End: Scoring
walkthrough.text: After the last adventure card, it is time to score. First, arrival bonuses: the leader gets the most credits, decreasing for each subsequent player. Second, the player with the fewest exposed connectors gets a best-looking ship bonus. Ties share the bonus. Third, sell all your goods based on the price list: yellow equals 1, green equals 2, blue equals 3, and red equals 4 credits. Fourth, lose 1 credit for each component in your discard pile, up to the round's maximum penalty. For Class I ships, the max penalty is 5 credits. Class II is 8, and Class III is 11.

walkthrough.title: End of Round and Next Round
walkthrough.text: After scoring, return all tokens, blocks, and figures to the bank. Take all your ship components except the starting pilot cabin and put them back in the Warehouse pile face down. Keep your cosmic credits. In Round 2, everyone takes a Class II ship board with a bigger building area. In Round 3, you take the massive Class III boards. The adventure decks get tougher too, with more cards and more dangerous events. The player who arrived first in the previous round says 'Go!' to start the next round.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends after all three rounds of building, flying, and scoring. Add up all your cosmic credits across all three rounds. If your total is 1 or more, congratulations, you made a profit! The player with the most cosmic credits is the ultimate winner. If you somehow end up with zero or negative credits, well, at least you survived the trip.

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three example players. Player 1 is cautious and methodical. Player 2 is a speed demon who rushes through building. Player 3 tries to build the biggest, most loaded ship possible. We will cover building, launching, and flying through several adventures so you can see how the game flows. Ready? Let's build some spaceships.

walkthrough.title: The Warehouse Is Open
walkthrough.text: Everyone has their Class I ship board with the pilot cabin in the center. The Warehouse is a big face-down pile of components in the middle of the table. Number tiles 1, 2, and 3 are in a row. Someone shouts 'Go!' and all three players start grabbing components simultaneously. Remember: one hand grabs, one hand stays above your board, and you can only hold one component at a time.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 Builds Carefully
walkthrough.text: Player 1 grabs a component, flips it over, and sees an engine. Perfect, engines are important. They carefully check that the exhaust pipe faces the rear of their ship and that there is nothing behind it. They attach it below the pilot cabin using a matching connector. Next they grab a cannon and place it on top, barrel pointing forward with nothing in front of it. Player 1 is taking their time but making solid, legal connections every time.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 Rushes and Makes a Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 2 is grabbing components as fast as possible. They slap down a cargo hold, then a cannon, then another cabin, barely checking connectors. But wait. They just placed a component with a simple connector right next to a double connector. That is an illegal connection. In their rush, they did not notice. They keep building on top of it. This is going to be a problem during the spot check.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Check Your Connectors
walkthrough.text: This is the number one mistake new players make: not checking connector types before placing a piece. Simple connectors look like a single pipe. Double connectors look like two pipes side by side. Universal connectors are a special circle shape. Simple cannot join double, ever. It takes just a second to check, and it saves you from losing components during the spot check. Slow down for half a second and check every touching side before you commit.

walkthrough.title: Player 3 Grabs Everything
walkthrough.text: Player 3 is methodically building a wide ship. They grab a battery, then a double cannon, then another battery, then a shield generator. Their ship is growing in all directions. They are trying to cover every square on the board. Meanwhile, Player 1 has noticed some nice face-up components that Player 3 returned and grabs a cargo hold with 3 containers.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 Finishes First
walkthrough.text: Player 2 declares they are done and grabs tile number 1. They will launch first! Their ship is small and scrappy looking, but they are confident. Player 1 finishes next and takes tile 2. Player 3 is still building. They finally run out of good spots and take tile 3. Now comes the spot check.

walkthrough.title: Spot Check: Player 2's Mistake Is Found
walkthrough.text: During the spot check, Player 1 notices Player 2's illegal connection: the simple connector next to a double. Player 2 has to remove the offending component and everything attached to it that becomes disconnected. Three components fall off and go into Player 2's discard pile. That is 3 credits of penalty at the end of the flight. Player 2's speed advantage just got expensive. In the full game, Player 2 would also have to give back their number tile and redistribute launch order.

walkthrough.title: Preparing Ships for Launch
walkthrough.text: Time to prepare for launch. Each player places 2 white human figures in every cabin, including the pilot cabin. Then place green battery tokens on each battery, 2 or 3 depending on the component. Player 1 has their pilot cabin plus 2 extra cabins for 6 total crew, 2 engines, 2 cannons, and a cargo hold. Player 2 has fewer components after the spot check but still has the pilot cabin and one extra cabin for 4 crew. Player 3 has the biggest ship with lots of batteries and shields. Ship markers go on the flight board.

walkthrough.title: Adventure 1: Open Space
walkthrough.text: The leader, Player 2 with tile 1, flips the first card: Open Space. Each player announces engine strength. Player 2 has only 1 simple engine, so strength 1, and moves forward 1 space. Player 1 has 2 simple engines for strength 2 and moves forward 2 spaces, passing Player 2. Player 3 has 1 simple engine and 1 double engine, spends a battery token, and gets strength 3. Player 3 zooms ahead. The order has already changed!

walkthrough.title: Adventure 2: Planets
walkthrough.text: Player 3 is now the leader and flips: Planets! There are 3 rows of goods. Player 3 goes first and picks the row with a red block, worth 4 credits but they need a special cargo hold. Good thing they built one. Player 1 picks the row with blue and green blocks. Player 2 is left with the last row of yellow blocks. Each player loads goods into their cargo containers and moves their marker back for the flight days spent landing.

walkthrough.title: Adventure 3: Meteoric Swarm
walkthrough.text: The new leader flips a Meteoric Swarm card. It shows a small meteor from the front and a large meteor from the front. For the small meteor, two dice are rolled: column 5. Player 1 checks column 5 on their ship. There is a component there but it has no exposed connector facing forward, so the meteor bounces harmlessly off. Player 2 checks: their column 5 has an exposed connector facing forward. They do not have a shield on that side. The component is destroyed and goes to the discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Second Mistake: No Shields
walkthrough.text: Player 2 built their ship with no shield generators at all. They were so focused on being fast that they skipped defensive components. Now every small meteor that hits an exposed connector is automatic damage. Shields cost just 1 battery token to use and can save critical components. Building without any shields is like driving without a seatbelt. You might be fine, but when something goes wrong, it goes very wrong.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Balanced Ships
walkthrough.text: The takeaway from Player 2's rough flight: speed in building is great, but not at the expense of a functional ship. You need a minimum of engines for movement, cannons for defense, cargo holds for income, and ideally shields for protection. A ship that launches first but falls apart during the flight will earn fewer credits than a solid ship that launches second or third. Balance speed with quality.

walkthrough.title: Adventure 4: Smugglers Attack
walkthrough.text: The leader flips an enemy card: Smugglers with strength 4. Player 3 is in the lead and gets attacked first. They count their cannons: 2 simple forward cannons plus 1 double cannon. They spend a battery token for the double cannon. That is 2 plus 2 equals 4. A tie. Nothing happens to Player 3, but the smugglers are not defeated. They move on to Player 1, who has 2 forward simple cannons for strength 2. Not enough. Player 1 loses their 2 most valuable goods. Ouch.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Wasting Batteries
walkthrough.text: Player 3 spent a battery token on the double cannon to tie with the smugglers. But a tie means nothing happens. They wasted a battery for no benefit. If they could not beat the enemy outright, they should have saved the battery. You cannot partially win a fight. Either your cannon strength exceeds the enemy and you win, or it does not and you gain nothing. Always count your maximum possible strength before deciding to spend batteries.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Do the Math Before Spending Batteries
walkthrough.text: Batteries are a limited resource that power your double engines, double cannons, and shields. Before spending one, ask yourself: will this actually change the outcome? If you cannot beat the enemy even with the battery, save it for a shield or for Open Space. Every wasted battery is a defense or speed boost you will not have later. Count your strength first, then decide.

walkthrough.title: Adventure 5: Combat Zone
walkthrough.text: A Combat Zone card appears. First line: the player with the fewest crew loses 3 flight days. Player 2 has only 2 crew left after losing a cabin to the meteor. They fall behind. Second line: the player with the weakest engines loses 2 crew. Player 2 again, with only 1 engine. They lose 2 of their remaining crew. That leaves Player 2 with zero humans. Player 2 must give up and is out of the round.

walkthrough.title: Adventures 6-8 and Journey's End
walkthrough.text: Player 1 and Player 3 continue through the remaining adventures. Another Open Space card lets Player 3 surge ahead. A Stardust event costs Player 1 some flight days for exposed connectors. When the last card is resolved, it is time to score. Player 3 arrived first and gets the top arrival bonus of 4 credits. Player 1 gets 3 credits for second place. Player 3 also wins the best-looking ship bonus for having fewer exposed connectors.

walkthrough.title: Selling Goods and Penalties
walkthrough.text: Both players sell their goods. Player 3 has a red block worth 4, a green block worth 2, and a yellow block worth 1, for 7 credits total. Player 1 lost their most valuable goods to the smugglers but still sells 3 credits worth. Now penalties: Player 1 lost 1 component during the flight for minus 1 credit. Player 3 lost none. Player 2 gave up and sells their remaining goods at half price. After everything is tallied, credits are kept face down for the next round.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen the complete flow of a Galaxy Trucker round. You watched Player 2 learn that rushing leads to illegal ships and missing defenses. You saw Player 3 learn that batteries should not be wasted on ties. And you saw how a balanced ship with engines, cannons, cargo, and shields gives you the best shot at a profitable journey. Two more rounds to go, with bigger ships and tougher adventures. You are ready!

walkthrough.title: Before You Launch
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for building priorities and common traps to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every component type and adventure card.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun out there, trucker!

summary.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 example players demonstrating building and flying
Player 1: careful builder, Player 2: speed demon, Player 3: maximizer
Covers one complete round from 'Go!' to scoring

summary.title: The Warehouse Is Open
bullets: All players grab simultaneously when someone shouts 'Go!'
One hand grabs, one stays above your board
Number tiles 1-3 ready for claiming when done

summary.title: Player 1 Builds Carefully
bullets: Checks engine exhaust faces rear, nothing behind it
Cannon barrel points forward, nothing in front
Takes time but makes solid, legal connections every time

summary.title: Player 2 Rushes and Makes a Mistake
bullets: Slaps down components without checking connector types
Places simple connector next to double connector (illegal!)
Keeps building on top of the illegal connection

summary.title: Lesson: Check Your Connectors
bullets: #1 mistake: not checking connector types before placing
Simple (single pipe) cannot join double (two pipes)
Universal (circle) joins anything
Check every touching side before committing a piece

summary.title: Player 3 Grabs Everything
bullets: Builds a wide ship with batteries, double cannon, shields
Methodically covers the board
Player 1 grabs useful face-up components others returned

summary.title: Player 2 Finishes First
bullets: Player 2 grabs tile 1 (launches first)
Player 1 takes tile 2, Player 3 takes tile 3
Now comes the spot check

summary.title: Spot Check: Player 2's Mistake Is Found
bullets: Illegal simple+double connection discovered
3 components removed to discard pile = 3 credits penalty later
Speed advantage wiped out by illegal construction

summary.title: Preparing Ships for Launch
bullets: 2 white humans per cabin, green battery tokens per battery
Ship markers on flight board: tile 1 in front, 2 spaces between each
Player 1: 6 crew, 2 engines, 2 cannons; Player 2: 4 crew (reduced); Player 3: big ship with shields

summary.title: Adventure 1: Open Space
bullets: Each player announces engine strength and moves forward
Player 2: strength 1, Player 1: strength 2, Player 3: strength 3 (spent battery on double engine)
Flight order changes — Player 3 takes the lead

summary.title: Adventure 2: Planets
bullets: Leader picks first, then in order; 1 player per planet row
Player 3 grabs red goods (4 credits), needs special cargo hold
Landing costs flight days — markers move backward

summary.title: Adventure 3: Meteoric Swarm
bullets: Roll 2 dice per meteor to determine row/column
Small meteor bounces off Player 1 (no exposed connector)
Player 2 loses a component — exposed connector with no shield

summary.title: Player 2's Second Mistake: No Shields
bullets: Built no shield generators — every exposed connector is vulnerable
Shields cost just 1 battery token and protect two sides
Speed without defense = fragile ship that falls apart

summary.title: Lesson: Build Balanced Ships
bullets: Need engines, cannons, cargo, AND shields
A solid ship launching 2nd beats a fragile ship launching 1st
Balance speed in building with ship quality

summary.title: Adventure 4: Smugglers Attack
bullets: Smugglers strength 4, attack leader first
Player 3 ties at 4 — nothing happens, smugglers move on
Player 1 has strength 2, loses 2 most valuable goods

summary.title: Player 3's Mistake: Wasting Batteries
bullets: Spent a battery token just to tie (no benefit from a tie)
Should have saved the battery for shields or Open Space later
Tying an enemy = same result as not spending at all

summary.title: Lesson: Do the Math Before Spending Batteries
bullets: Batteries power double engines, double cannons, and shields — limited supply
Only spend if it changes the outcome (win vs. tie, block vs. take hit)
Count max strength first, then decide

summary.title: Adventure 5: Combat Zone
bullets: Fewest crew = lose flight days, weakest engines = lose crew, weakest cannons = get shot
Player 2 hit hardest — fewest crew and weakest engines
Loses all humans and must give up

summary.title: Adventures 6-8 and Journey's End
bullets: Player 1 and Player 3 finish remaining adventures
Player 3 arrives first (4 credits) and wins best-looking ship bonus
Player 1 gets 3 credits for second place

summary.title: Selling Goods and Penalties
bullets: Player 3 sells 7 credits of goods, Player 1 sells 3 credits
Player 1 loses 1 credit for lost component
Player 2 gave up — sells goods at half price, no arrival bonus

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Seen complete round: build, spot check, launch, adventures, scoring
Player 2 learned: don't rush, check connectors, build defenses
Player 3 learned: don't waste batteries on ties
Balanced ships with engines, cannons, cargo, shields win the day

summary.title: Before You Launch
bullets: Check out General Tips for building priorities and common traps
Reference the Appendix for component types and adventure cards
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, let's cover the traps that catch every new Galaxy Trucker player. First, mismatching connectors. Simple and double cannot connect, period. Check every side. Second, placing components behind engines or in front of cannons. Those squares must stay empty. Third, building a ship with no shields. One bad meteor roll on an exposed connector and you lose a critical component. Fourth, spending all your batteries early in the flight and having none left for emergencies. Fifth, ignoring exposed connectors. Each one is a vulnerability to small meteors and costs you flight days during Stardust events. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the worst disasters.

walkthrough.title: Building Priorities
walkthrough.text: When the Warehouse opens, here is what to prioritize. First, grab engines. You need at least 2 for a Class I ship, preferably 3. Speed determines flight position, which determines who gets first pick at planets and who gets attacked first by enemies. Second, get at least 2 forward-facing cannons. Enemies and large meteors require cannon strength, and forward-facing cannons count at full value while side ones count at half. Third, grab cargo holds for income, you need goods to make money. Fourth, batteries to power double components and shields. Fifth, shield generators to cover all four sides if possible. Structural modules are filler but they help hold your ship together.

walkthrough.title: The Speed vs. Quality Tradeoff
walkthrough.text: Finishing first gives you tile 1, which means launching in front. Being in front is powerful. You get first pick at planets, first chance at abandoned ships and stations, and enemies might be defeated before they reach you. But a ship that launches first and falls apart during the flight will earn fewer credits than a solid ship launching third. The sweet spot is building a good enough ship fast. Do not try to fill every square. A compact, well-connected ship with the right mix of components will outperform a sprawling mess. Know when to stop and grab your tile.

walkthrough.title: Protect Your Ship's Core
walkthrough.text: When building, think about structural integrity. Your pilot cabin is in the center. If you lose it, you are probably done. Build outward from the center with strong connections, preferably universal or double connectors. Avoid having critical components like your only engine or your only cannon connected by a single exposed connector on the edge of your ship. If a meteor hits that connector, you lose the component and anything beyond it. Think of your ship like a tree. The trunk should be thick and strong, with branches that can break off without killing the tree.

walkthrough.title: Battery Management
walkthrough.text: Batteries are your most versatile resource. They power double engines for speed, double cannons for combat, and shields for defense. But each battery cell can only be used once. A 3-cell battery gives you three uses across the entire flight. Do not spend batteries unless the outcome matters. If you cannot beat an enemy even with a double cannon, do not waste the battery. If a small meteor will only hit a structural module, maybe let it go instead of burning a shield charge. Save batteries for large meteor defense, crucial Open Space movement, and the inevitable Combat Zone.

walkthrough.title: Minimize Exposed Connectors
walkthrough.text: Exposed connectors, any connector that does not join to another component, are a liability in three ways. First, small meteors only damage your ship if they hit an exposed connector. Second, the Stardust event costs you 1 flight day per exposed connector. Third, the player with the fewest exposed connectors earns a bonus at the end of the flight. To minimize them, try to build your ship as a compact block rather than a long line. Use structural modules to fill gaps. Even placing a component with smooth sides next to exposed connectors can help, as long as it makes a legal connection somewhere.

walkthrough.title: Peeking at Adventure Cards
walkthrough.text: In the full game, three piles of adventure cards are placed face up on the flight board before building starts. You can pick up a pile and look through it at any time during building. This is incredibly valuable. If you see lots of planets, build more cargo holds. If you see a Combat Zone, make sure you have strong engines and cannons. If you see Epidemic, avoid connecting cabins to each other. If you see lots of enemies, stack up on forward cannons. The trade-off is that looking at cards takes time you could spend building. Experienced players grab a quick look early and adjust their strategy on the fly.

walkthrough.title: Goods and the Price List
walkthrough.text: Making money from goods is often the biggest income source in a round. Yellow goods are worth 1 credit, green are worth 2, blue are worth 3, and red are worth 4. Red goods are hazardous and can only go in special cargo holds with reinforced containers. When loading, always keep the most valuable goods. If you have to dump excess, dump the cheapest ones. When rearranging, put red goods in special holds and fill normal holds with whatever fits. Having empty cargo space is a missed opportunity, so build enough holds to capitalize on planet landings.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Mismatching connectors: simple cannot join double
Placing components behind engines or in front of cannons
Building with no shields — exposed connectors become death traps
Spending all batteries early, leaving none for emergencies
Ignoring exposed connectors — Stardust costs 1 flight day each

2nd: Forward-facing cannons (at least 2) — full value vs. half for side
3rd: Cargo holds — goods are your main income
4th: Batteries — power doubles and shields
5th: Shield generators — cover all 4 sides ideally

summary.title: The Speed vs. Quality Tradeoff
bullets: Tile 1 = launch first, first pick at planets, enemies may be beaten before you
But a fragile ship launching 1st earns less than a solid ship launching 3rd
Build 'good enough' fast — don't try to fill every square
Know when to stop building and grab your tile

summary.title: Protect Your Ship's Core
bullets: Build outward from pilot cabin with strong connections
Use universal/double connectors near the center
Avoid critical components on thin, breakable branches
Think tree trunk: thick core, expendable edges

summary.title: Battery Management
bullets: Each battery cell = 1 use (double engine, double cannon, or shield)
Don't spend if outcome doesn't change (e.g., tie vs. loss)
Save for: large meteor defense, crucial Open Space, Combat Zone
Let minor hits through if the component is expendable

summary.title: Minimize Exposed Connectors
bullets: Exposed connectors: vulnerable to small meteors, Stardust penalty, no ship bonus
Build compact blocks, not long lines
Use structural modules to fill gaps
Fewest exposed connectors = bonus credits at journey's end

summary.title: Peeking at Adventure Cards
bullets: Full game: 3 face-up card piles available during building
See planets? Build more cargo holds. See enemies? Build cannons.
See Epidemic? Don't connect cabins. See Combat Zone? Build engines.
Trade-off: looking at cards costs building time

summary.title: Goods and the Price List
bullets: Yellow=1cr, Green=2cr, Blue=3cr, Red=4cr (special holds only)
Always keep most valuable goods when forced to dump
Build enough cargo holds to capitalize on planet landings
Empty cargo space = missed income opportunity

walkthrough.title: The Speed Demon Strategy
walkthrough.text: One advanced approach is to build a small, efficient ship as fast as possible to guarantee tile 1. With the best launch position, you are first to every planet, first at every abandoned ship and station, and enemies might be defeated by other players before reaching you. The key is building a compact ship with 2 to 3 engines, 2 forward cannons, 1 to 2 cargo holds, and a shield generator in under two minutes. Skip the double components if you do not have time for batteries. Accept that your ship will lose components and keep the maximum penalty low. This strategy works best in Rounds 1 and 2 where the flight is shorter.

walkthrough.title: The Fortress Strategy
walkthrough.text: The opposite approach: take your time and build an indestructible ship. Load up on shields covering all four sides, batteries to power them, structural modules for redundancy, and universal connectors everywhere. The goal is to arrive at the destination with zero lost components and win the best-looking ship bonus. The trade-off is launching last, meaning other players grab the best planet rows and opportunities before you. This strategy works best in Round 3 where the longer flight and harsher adventures punish fragile ships, and the arrival bonuses and penalties are larger.

walkthrough.title: The Cargo King Strategy
walkthrough.text: Goods are often the single largest source of income. The cargo-focused approach prioritizes cargo holds and special cargo holds above everything else. Build as much storage as possible, including special holds for those valuable 4-credit red goods. You will need decent engines to stay near the front for first pick at planets. The downside is less room for cannons and shields, making you vulnerable to enemies and meteors. Mitigate this by peeking at adventure cards during building. If you see lots of planets and few enemies, go all-in on cargo.

walkthrough.title: The Gunship Strategy
walkthrough.text: Some flights are packed with enemies. The gunship strategy stacks forward-facing cannons and double cannons to dominate combat. When you defeat enemies, you claim their rewards, which can include cosmic credits and valuable goods. Other players behind you are safe because defeated enemies do not attack further. This makes you popular but also means you are spending batteries on double cannons. Pair this with batteries and a purple alien for plus 2 cannon strength. In Round 3, pirates can reward huge credit payouts, making this approach very profitable.

walkthrough.title: Using Aliens Effectively
walkthrough.text: Aliens are powerful but costly. Each takes up a full cabin that could hold 2 humans, so you trade 2 crew for 1 alien. The purple alien gives plus 2 cannon strength, which is like having 2 extra forward cannons for free. The orange alien gives plus 2 engine strength, equivalent to 2 extra engines. But these bonuses only work if your base strength is already at least 1. The best use of aliens is to push already-decent ships into dominant territory. If you have 3 cannons and a purple alien, you effectively have strength 5, which beats most enemies outright. Build the life support module adjacent to a cabin, not isolated.

walkthrough.title: Timer Manipulation
walkthrough.text: In the full game, the sand timer controls building time. When it runs out, anyone can flip it to the next circle. This means a fast builder can flip the timer early to pressure slower opponents. If you finish first and grab tile 1, you want to flip the timer to the Start circle as soon as possible to force everyone else to stop building with half-finished ships. Conversely, if you are behind, try to time your building so you are nearly done when the final flip happens. Experienced groups turn timer flipping into a psychological weapon. Build fast, flip early, watch your opponents scramble.

walkthrough.title: Setting Components Aside
walkthrough.text: In the full game, you can set aside up to 2 components in your discard area during building. These are reserved for you and cannot be taken by other players. The downside is that if you do not add them to your ship, they count as lost components and you pay the penalty. Use this strategically: if you find a perfect double engine but do not have space for it yet, set it aside while you build a battery nearby. Just remember the risk. Experienced players set aside high-value components early and build their ship to accommodate them, rather than the other way around.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Adventure Cards
walkthrough.text: In the full game, three piles of adventure cards are visible during building, and a fourth unknown pile is added afterward. The cards you can see tell you roughly what dangers and opportunities to expect. If you see two Planets cards, build extra cargo. If you see a Combat Zone and enemy cards, prioritize cannons and crew. If you see Epidemic, never connect two cabins together. The fourth pile adds uncertainty, but knowing three-quarters of the flight gives you a massive edge in ship design. Experienced players spend the first 30 seconds peeking at all three piles before grabbing a single component.

walkthrough.title: Round-by-Round Scaling
walkthrough.text: Each round demands different building priorities. In Round 1, Class I ships are tiny and the flight is short, so simple engines and cannons work fine. Focus on speed and getting tile 1. In Round 2, Class II ships are bigger and the flight is longer with tougher adventures. You need double components and batteries to stay competitive. In Round 3, Class III ships are massive and the flight is brutal with Epidemic and Sabotage possible. Build for durability with redundant connections, shields on all sides, and separated cabins. The max penalty also scales, from 5 in Round 1 to 8 in Round 2 to 11 in Round 3, so losing components hurts more in later rounds.

walkthrough.title: When to Give Up
walkthrough.text: Sometimes giving up is the best strategy. If your ship is heavily damaged early in the flight, the remaining adventures will only make it worse. Every lost component adds penalty credits, and if you lose all your goods, there is nothing left to sell. By giving up early, you lock in half-price goods sales and avoid further penalties. The key calculation is whether your expected future income from arrival bonus and new goods exceeds the expected future losses from component penalties and lost cargo. In Round 3, with max penalty of 11 credits, a badly damaged ship can easily go negative. Know when to cut your losses and save your credits for the final score.

entries.definition: Your starting component placed in the center of the ship board. Has 4 universal connectors, making it the easiest piece to build around. Holds 2 human crew members. Cannot hold aliens even with a life support system.

A component that houses crew members. Each cabin holds 2 white human figures. If joined to a life support system, it can instead hold 1 alien of the matching color. More cabins mean more crew for Combat Zones and Abandoned Stations.

A propulsion component that adds 1 to your engine strength. The exhaust pipe must point toward the rear of the ship (toward the player). No component can be placed on the square directly behind the engine exhaust.

A high-powered propulsion component that adds 2 to engine strength but requires spending 1 green battery token each time it is used. Follows the same placement rules as simple engines: exhaust toward rear, nothing behind it.

A weapon component that adds 1 to cannon strength when pointing forward, or 1/2 when pointing to the side or rear. No component can be placed on the square directly in front of the cannon barrel. Used to defend against large meteors and enemies.

A high-powered weapon that adds 2 to cannon strength when pointing forward (or 1 when pointing to the side or rear) but requires spending 1 green battery token per use. Follows the same placement rules as simple cannons.

An energy storage component with 2 or 3 E-cells. Each cell stores enough energy for one use of a double engine, double cannon, or shield generator. Place green tokens on the component during setup. Can be placed anywhere on the ship.

A defensive component that protects two sides of the ship from small meteors and light cannon fire. The two glowing green sides indicate which directions are protected. Costs 1 green battery token per activation. Two properly oriented generators can cover all four sides.

A storage component with 2 or 3 containers for goods. Each container holds 1 colored block. Can store yellow, green, blue, or red goods. Red goods (hazardous) cannot be stored in normal cargo holds — they require special holds.

A reinforced storage component with 1 or 2 containers. Can hold any type of goods including hazardous red goods. Red goods are worth 4 credits each, the most valuable, so special holds are essential for maximizing income.

A component with no special function but lots of connectors, many of them universal. Helps hold the ship together and reduces exposed connectors. Acts as filler but improves structural integrity and the best-looking ship bonus.

An alien life support component that, when joined to a cabin, allows that cabin to hold 1 purple alien instead of 2 humans. The purple alien provides +2 cannon strength if your base cannon strength is at least 1.

An alien life support component that, when joined to a cabin, allows that cabin to hold 1 orange alien instead of 2 humans. The orange alien provides +2 engine strength if your base engine strength is at least 1.
A single-pipe connector on the edge of a component. Can legally join with another simple connector or a universal connector. Cannot join with a double connector or a smooth side.
A two-pipe connector on the edge of a component. Can legally join with another double connector or a universal connector. Cannot join with a simple connector or a smooth side.

A special connector shown as a circular symbol. Can legally join with any other connector type: simple, double, or universal. Cannot join with a smooth side. Found on the pilot cabin and structural modules.
An edge of a component with no connector at all. Cannot join with any connector. Two smooth sides can sit next to each other as long as the piece makes a legal connection on at least one other side.

Any connector on a placed component that is not adjacent to another component. Exposed connectors make the ship vulnerable to small meteors, cause flight day losses during Stardust events, and reduce the best-looking ship bonus. Minimize them.

The central pile of face-down components that players grab from during the building phase. Returned components are placed face up and available for anyone to take. The Warehouse is shared by all players.

Tiles numbered 1 through 4 that players take when they finish building. The number determines launch order on the flight board. Tile 1 launches first (the leader). Lower numbers are better for opportunities but mean less building time.

Used in the full game to limit building time. Starts on a numbered circle matching the round. When it runs out, anyone can flip it to the next lower circle. After the final flip to Start, building ends when time runs out. Only a player with a number tile can do the final flip.

During building in the full game, you may reserve up to 2 components in your discard area. They cannot be taken by others but if not added to your ship, they count as lost components with a credit penalty at journey's end.

After building, players inspect each other's ships for illegal construction. In the full game, illegal components must be removed to the discard pile and count as lost. The player keeps their number tile but pays penalty credits for removed pieces.

The central game board with a circular track of triangular spaces. Ship markers move along the track during the flight. The center shows the price list for goods and the round's rules summary. Adventure card piles are placed on designated squares.

The player whose ship marker is farthest ahead on the flight board. The leader flips adventure cards, decides first on opportunities (planets, abandoned ships/stations), and gets attacked first by enemies. Leadership can change during the flight.

Movement on the flight board measured in empty spaces. Gaining flight days moves your marker forward. Losing flight days moves it backward. Landing on planets, losing to Stardust, and taking opportunities all cost flight days. Occupied spaces are skipped when counting.
The total propulsion power of your ship. Each simple engine = 1, each powered double engine = 2, orange alien = +2 (if base > 0). Used during Open Space cards and Combat Zone evaluations.

The total firepower of your ship. Each forward simple cannon = 1, forward double = 2 (costs 1 battery). Side/rear cannons count at half value. Purple alien = +2 (if base > 0). Used against enemies and in Combat Zones.

Human figures (white) and alien figures (purple/orange) on your ship. Humans occupy cabins at 2 per cabin. Crew count matters for Combat Zones, Abandoned Stations, and Slavers encounters. Losing all humans forces you to give up.
An adventure card that lets ships race ahead. Each player announces engine strength (in order from leader) and moves forward that many empty spaces. Faster ships gain better flight position.
An adventure card showing 2-4 rows of goods. Players choose in order (leader first) to land on one planet row. Only 1 player per row. Landing costs flight days but lets you load goods onto your ship.

An adventure card where one player can trade a specified number of crew members for cosmic credits. The leader decides first, then the opportunity passes down. Costs flight days. Only one player can claim it.

An adventure card where one player can dock to gain goods if they have at minimum the required crew count. Leader decides first. Costs flight days. Unlike Abandoned Ship, you do not lose crew members.

An adventure card depicting multiple meteors from specific directions. For each meteor, 2 dice are rolled to determine the row or column of impact. Small meteors damage exposed connectors; large meteors destroy the hit component and can only be stopped by cannons.

A meteor that bounces harmlessly off a well-built ship. Only damages if it hits an exposed connector facing the direction the meteor comes from. Can be blocked by a shield on that side (costs 1 battery token).

A meteor that destroys whatever component it hits regardless of construction quality. Shields cannot stop it. The only defense is a cannon pointing at it in the same column (for front/back meteors) or same row/adjacent row (for side meteors).

An enemy type that attacks ships in order from the leader. If your cannon strength exceeds theirs, you win their goods. If you lose, you must give up your most valuable goods. If you tie, nothing happens. Defeated smugglers stop attacking.

An enemy type encountered in later rounds. If your cannon strength exceeds theirs, you earn cosmic credits. If defeated by slavers, you must surrender crew members of your choice. Available in Rounds 2 and 3.

An enemy type encountered in Round 3. If your cannon strength exceeds theirs, you earn cosmic credits. If defeated, your ship takes cannon fire (light and/or heavy) with dice rolls determining impact location.

An adventure card with 3 penalty lines. Fewest crew = lose flight days. Weakest engines = lose crew. Weakest cannons = take cannon fire from behind. Ties: player farthest ahead pays. Tests all aspects of ship design.

A special event (yellow card) that causes every player to lose 1 flight day per exposed connector on their ship. Evaluated from last player to first. A strong incentive to minimize exposed connectors during building.
A special event in Rounds 2-3. Removes 1 crew member from every occupied cabin that is joined directly to another occupied cabin. Building ships with separated cabins avoids this entirely.

A special event in Round 3. The ship with the smallest crew has a random component destroyed via dice roll for coordinates. If 3 rolls miss the ship, the saboteur gives up. Can cause devastating chain reactions.
The least valuable cargo type, worth 1 cosmic credit when sold. Can be stored in any cargo hold (normal or special). Represented by yellow blocks.
Cargo worth 2 cosmic credits when sold. Can be stored in any cargo hold (normal or special). Represented by green blocks.
Cargo worth 3 cosmic credits when sold. Can be stored in any cargo hold (normal or special). Represented by blue blocks.
The most valuable cargo type, worth 4 cosmic credits when sold. Hazardous materials that can only be stored in special cargo holds with reinforced containers. Represented by red blocks.

The currency and victory points of Galaxy Trucker. Earned from arrival bonuses, selling goods, defeating enemies, and abandoned ships. Lost from component penalties. Kept face down between rounds. Most credits after Round 3 wins.

Credits awarded based on flight position when the last adventure card is resolved. The leader gets the most, decreasing for each subsequent player. Players who gave up get no arrival bonus. Values vary by round.
Credits awarded to the player with the fewest exposed connectors at journey's end. If tied, all tied players receive the bonus. Encourages compact, well-connected ship design.

At journey's end, you lose 1 cosmic credit for each component in your discard pile (lost during flight or spot check). There is a maximum penalty per round: 5 for Class I, 8 for Class II, 11 for Class III. Class IIIa has no maximum.

Withdrawing from the current round's flight. You sell goods at half price, get no arrival bonus, and are removed from the flight board. Must give up if all humans are lost, engine strength is 0 during Open Space, or you are lapped by the leader.

An alien crew member that provides +2 cannon strength if your base cannon strength is at least 1. Takes up a full cabin (replacing 2 humans). Requires a purple life support system joined to the cabin. Max 1 per ship.

An alien crew member that provides +2 engine strength if your base engine strength is at least 1. Takes up a full cabin (replacing 2 humans). Requires an orange life support system joined to the cabin. Max 1 per ship.

White figures placed 2 per cabin (including pilot cabin). Do not require life support systems. Losing all human crew forces you to give up the round. Crew count matters for Combat Zones, Abandoned Stations, and defense against Slavers.

The smallest ship board used in Round 1. Has a compact building grid centered on the 'I' starting square. Maximum component loss penalty: 5 credits. Shortest flight with the least dangerous adventures.

The medium ship board used in Round 2. Larger building grid than Class I with the 'II' starting square. Maximum component loss penalty: 8 credits. Longer flight with enemies including Slavers and events including Epidemic.

The largest ship board used in Round 3. Massive building grid with the 'III' starting square. Maximum component loss penalty: 11 credits. Longest, most dangerous flight with Pirates, Epidemic, and Sabotage possible.

An optional variant ship board for an extra Round 3a with experienced players. Has no discard area, meaning there is no maximum penalty — you pay for every lost component. The most challenging flight in the game.

A type of attack from enemies or Combat Zones. Roll 2 dice to determine row/column of impact. Can be blocked by a shield generator oriented to protect that direction (costs 1 battery token). If not blocked, the hit component is destroyed.

The most dangerous attack type. Roll 2 dice to determine row/column of impact. Cannot be blocked by shields or any other defense. If it hits a component, that component is destroyed. Your only hope is rolling numbers that miss your ship entirely.
